summary: Andrew Carnegie believed that wealth should be systematically given away during one's lifetime to benefit society, rather than hoarded or passed to heirs. He developed a rigorous approach to 'scientific philanthropy,' focusing his fortune on institutions like libraries and universities that empowered individuals willing to help themselves, while actively avoiding charity that encouraged dependency.

principles.rationale: Wealth should be distributed while the benefactor is still alive to ensure it is directed purposefully. Leaving money in a will merely indicates the deceased could not take it with them.
principles.source_quote: Men who leave vast sums [in their wills] may fairly be thought men who would not have left it at all had they been able to take it with them.

principles.statement: Help those willing to help themselves.
principles.rationale: Philanthropy should provide the tools for self-improvement (like libraries) rather than handouts that encourage laziness or vice.
principles.source_quote: It were better for mankind that the millions of the rich were thrown into the sea than so spent as to encourage the slothful, the drunken, the unworthy.

principles.statement: Do not spoil your heirs with inherited wealth.
principles.rationale: Leaving vast fortunes to children is harmful to their character and acts more as a burden than a blessing.
principles.source_quote: I should as soon leave to my son a curse as the almighty dollar.

frameworks.name: Carnegie Library Donation Procedure
frameworks.when_to_apply: When a municipality requests funding to build a public library.
steps: Step One: Make the request in writing to Carnegie's New York home, including the population of the town.
Step Two: Provide the site, preferably land near the center of town.
Step Three: Pledge annual spending for maintenance, usually requiring the town to match 10 percent of the gift annually.
frameworks.source_quote: Ordering a library from Carnegie was as easy as ordering a sofa from the Sears Catalog.

mental_models.name: Scientific Philanthropy
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing charity not as emotional handouts, but as strategic, structured investments in institutions (like libraries, universities, and hospitals) that yield societal self-improvement and avoid wasteful spending.
mental_models.example: Carnegie refused to give money to individuals, instead funding 2,811 libraries because they appealed to his 'bootstrap sensibility for self-improvement.'

mental_models.name: Cooperation vs. Conflict (Rowing vs. Football)
mental_models.description: Using sports as a metaphor for global relations and human development. Conflict-based sports groom youth for violence, while cooperative sports model peaceful global relations.
mental_models.example: Carnegie funded a $400,000 lake at Princeton so the university could have a rowing crew, specifically to take young men's minds off football, which he felt groomed them for 'violence and deception.'

heuristics: The man who dies rich dies disgraced.

signature_quotes.text: The man who dies rich dies disgraced.
signature_quotes.context: Carnegie's guiding philosophy on wealth and philanthropy.

signature_quotes.text: Of every thousand dollars spent in so-called charity today, it is probable that nine hundred and fifty dollars is unwisely spent.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1889, explaining his need for 'scientific philanthropy.'

signature_quotes.text: I should as soon leave to my son a curse as the almighty dollar.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his belief that inherited wealth spoils heirs.

signature_quotes.text: It were better for mankind that the millions of the rich were thrown into the sea than so spent as to encourage the slothful, the drunken, the unworthy.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why charity must only help those willing to help themselves.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Giving money without requiring effort from the recipient encourages the 'slothful, the drunken, the unworthy' rather than promoting self-improvement.
Leaving vast sums in a will implies the giver would have kept the money if they could have taken it with them, rather than demonstrating true generosity.
Passing down massive fortunes spoils the heirs and acts as a 'curse' rather than a benefit.
